steps.text: Provide CDC with documentation of a plan with a timeline for substantive involvement in, and development of, the content of the meeting, including standards that ensure scientific and public health merit.
Provide a written process of how presentations or abstracts are to be reviewed and criteria that will be used for selecting presentations.
Agree to abide by CDC Guiding Principles for the Conduct of Scientific Conferences, when applicable.

Establish a written process for approving conference funding, including information on how proposals will be solicited, elements required in a proposal, and a process for review and approval by the CC/CO/NC.
Determine the appropriate funding instrument (acquisition award for federal conferences with direct federal benefit, or assistance award for non-federal conferences supporting a public purpose).
Identify the conference prior to the award when supported by assistance awards other than Conference Grants.
Request and obtain prior approval from the Grants Management Officer if the conference is proposed post-award.
Include the specified conference support funding language (Reference 7D) in all program announcements for CDC sponsorship of nonfederal conferences when funds are to be awarded.
Submit a formal request in writing to the CCs/COs/NCs having related programmatic responsibility and/or the Office of the Director (OD), CDC.
Review and approve or disapprove the request.
Coordinate requests made to the OD, CDC promptly with the director(s) of the CCs/COs/NC(s) having programmatic responsibility.
Provide a written response to the conference organizer(s) indicating disapproval of the request.
Provide a written response to the conference organizer(s) indicating tentative approval.
Review the full final conference agenda and other conference information.
Provide a written response to the conference organizer(s) indicating final approval or disapproval of sponsorship.
Complete a co-sponsorship agreement with the non-federal co-sponsor.
Obtain written certification from management of the proposed meeting facility stating that facilities, goods, and services are fully ADA compliant.
Complete the Accessible Meetings Checklist and keep it on file with other conference documentation.
Approve the conference sponsorship request or issue obligation documents.
Maintain a copy of all written correspondence regarding conferences that CDC is asked to sponsor on file in the Office of the Director of the CC/CO/NC having programmatic responsibility.
Include documentation required as per Section 4H in the records.
Account fully and accurately for each party’s programmatic and financial responsibilities and activities in agreements and records.
Describe separately in agreements and records any discrete portion of an event that will be exclusively sponsored by CDC or exclusively sponsored by a non-Federal entity.
Make agreements and records concerning the amounts, sources, and uses of funds available to the public upon request.
Assign a specific person responsibility for maintaining these records, and for submitting a quarterly line listing of all conferences sponsored or disapproved to the OD, CDC.
Maintain records of these listings at the OD and make them available to the CC/CO/NCs upon request.
Determine how the potential collaboration fits within CDC's overall mission and priorities and the private partner's mission and priorities.
Address independence and objectivity of scientific judgment in the potential collaboration and identify mechanisms to ensure objectivity and independence.
Assess the effect of the private partner's products or services, if applicable, on health and whether they are compatible with CDC's mission.

Assess the behavior of the private partner in conducting business and determine whether the partner's behavior is consistent with CDC's mission and the principles guiding private sector collaboration.
Assess the public impact of the partner's broad public mission and image.
Seek multiple collaborators that represent a broad spectrum of opinions and interests.
Inform private partners that the existence of the collaborative relationship will be made a matter of public record.
Work out early how the public record disclosure will occur.
Establish a formal review and advisory process within each CC, CO, or NC for examining potential collaborations.
Ensure employees developing collaborations with the private sector receive orientation and guidance from the CDC Ethics Officer.

preconditions.text: The conference should support CDC's mission and further CDC's mission of promoting health and quality of life by preventing and controlling disease, injury, and disability.
Scientific meetings sponsored by CDC must meet the Guiding Principles for the Conduct of Scientific Conferences (when applicable to the conference format and purpose).
Prior to receiving funding (for external conference organizers)
For conferences/meetings funded directly by the CCs/COs/NCs
CDC must approve the sponsorship if CDC is to be identified as a sponsor of the event when grantees use general program funds.
The conference must be within the authorized programmatic scope and integral to successful completion of the specific project to be supported by assistance awards other than Conference Grants.
Sponsorship of a conference is requested and does not involve expenditure of CDC funds.
The CC/CO/NC or the OD, CDC, does not wish to sponsor the conference.
The CC/CO/NC is interested in sponsoring the conference.
ADA compliance for the meeting facility has been previously assured, the documentation is maintained by the CC/CO/NC, and the facility has not undergone any renovation changing its accessibility.
The meeting facility has not undergone any renovation that would change the accessibility of the facility.

constraints.text: Scientific meetings and conferences should be unbiased forums for exchange of scientific information and consider the implications for public health.
Scientific meetings and conferences should provide venues for open scientific discourse.
Organizers of scientific meetings and conferences should provide a transparent process for selecting presentations based primarily on scientific merit.
CDC sponsored conferences will be guided by sound scientific and programmatic oversight.
Organizers of scientific conferences will adhere to CDC Guiding Principles for the Conduct of Scientific Conferences when applicable.
The organizer is responsible for ensuring that CDC-sponsored conferences/meetings are managed in a transparent, objective, and unbiased manner.
CDC funds will not be expended for nonapproved portions of meetings.
Contingency awards will be made allowing usage of only 10% of the total amount.

All scientific conferences receiving CDC funds must meet CDC’s Guiding Principles for the Conduct of Scientific Conferences and follow relevant policies and procedures indicated under 'Securing Approval for Sponsorship of Conferences.'

The request must provide: the organization's mission, purpose of the conference, the organizer's demonstrated capacity, proposed content and format, intended audience, and other cosponsors, their mission, and their role.
No verbal disapproval of sponsorship may be provided.
The tentative approval response must contain specific language regarding CDC's active participation, rights to serve on planning committee, review content, and terminate sponsorship.
No verbal approval of sponsorship may be provided.
All conferences sponsored by CDC shall be held in facilities that are fully accessible to the public as required by American with Disabilities Act Accessibility Guidelines (ADAAG).

The written certification or documentation of the review of ADA obligations must address: adequate accessible parking; accessible entrance; doors at entrances; accessible door hardware; adequate wheelchair maneuvering space; accessible water and toilet facilities; accessible fixed seating and tables; accessible sales and service counters; and auxiliary aids and services.

A public accommodation must provide auxiliary aids and services (such as qualified interpreters, assistive listening headsets, television captioning, TDDs, videotext displays, readers, taped texts, Braille, or large print) when necessary to ensure effective communication.
CDC shall not co-sponsor an event with an entity that will not make information concerning funding publicly available.
For highly controversial issues, multiple collaborators should be sought that represent a broad spectrum of opinions and interests.
Avoid participating in indirect collaborations unless CDC would participate in a direct relationship with the partners.
No direct endorsement of a product or service is permitted by the agency.
CDC should maintain a process of openness and fairness in developing collaborations with partners on similar projects or projects with similar intent.
